title: Upstream Cache Failure Plan
prompt: Design an upstream API lookup path for a plugin that fetches remote records. Include the WordPress HTTP API call, transient caching, timeout/error behavior, cache key, and what the tool should return when the upstream service fails.
hints: A remote API can be slow, down, or surprising. The plugin should stay polite under all three conditions.

model_answer.summary: Wrap upstream lookups in the WordPress HTTP API, cache successful reads, and make failure output structured and recoverable.
model_answer.work: Build a stable cache key from the normalized lookup input, such as mcpoly_remote_record_ plus md5 of the request arguments. Check get_transient first and return cached data with status=cached when present. On a cache miss, call wp_remote_get with a short timeout and no secrets in the URL, validate wp_remote_retrieve_response_code, decode and validate the body, then set_transient with an explicit TTL such as HOUR_IN_SECONDS. If the request returns WP_Error, times out, gives a bad status code, or has invalid JSON, return structured output with status=error, error_code, note, recoverable=true, and stale cached data if a stale-safe fallback policy exists.
checks: Test cache hit and cache miss paths.
Simulate timeout, WP_Error, non-200 status code, and invalid JSON.
Verify cache key normalization avoids leaking secrets.
Verify callers receive structured recoverable output instead of a fatal error.

rubric_mapping.criterion: Uses WordPress HTTP and caching APIs

model_answer.why_this_is_good: It assumes remote services are unreliable and protects the WordPress site from slow, noisy, or secret-leaking failure modes.
common_misses: Calling the remote API on every page view.
No timeout or status code handling.
Caching errors as if they were real data.
Returning false or crashing instead of structured error output.
model_answer.revision_prompt: Revise by describing the cache key, TTL, HTTP timeout, response validation, and every failure response the caller can recover from.

criteria.name: Uses WordPress HTTP and caching APIs
